mnemonics: Objects are Passengers on a Return Trip: When you return an object, you're not creating a new one from scratch, you're just sending a passenger (a reference) back to the caller, who can then use it.
keyPoints: A method can return an object of any class type, not just primitives.
You are returning a reference to the object, not a copy of the object itself.
The returned object's methods can be called directly on the return value (e.g., `myMethod().doSomething()`).
commonMistakes: Forgetting that the returned object might be null and calling a method on it, leading to a NullPointerException.
Assuming that returning an object creates a copy, leading to unintended side-effects if the original object is modified.
